---
title: "Turing's two great ideas were the same idea"
description: "The 1936 universal computer, the 1952 morphogen equations, and Conway's 1970 Game of Life are one intellectual project at three resolutions."
author: "Steve Haigh"
date: "2026-05-27"
categories: [biology, computation, books]
draft: true
---

In 1936 Alan Turing wrote down what a computer is. In 1952 he wrote down what a leopard's spots are. The two papers are usually shelved in different rooms — theoretical computer science, theoretical biology — but the underlying move is the same one, and Conway's Game of Life is the bridge between them.

The 1936 paper asks: what is the minimal abstract machine that can compute anything computable? The answer is a head reading a tape, a finite set of states, a transition table. No physics, no biology, no semantics, just local rules iterated indefinitely. The state of the machine at time *t+1* is a function of the state at time *t*. Computation is what you get when you let it run.

The 1952 paper asks: how does a homogeneous ball of cells produce a non-homogeneous animal? Turing's answer is that two chemicals — morphogens — react with each other and diffuse across the substrate, and under particular ratios of reaction rate to diffusion rate the homogeneous solution becomes unstable. The chemicals self-organise into peaks and troughs. Stripes appear. Spots appear. No central designer, no global plan, just local rules iterated until they settle.

Read those two summaries next to each other. Same shape. Local rules, iterated in parallel, producing structure that looks designed.

Conway's Game of Life (1970) is the same idea at a third resolution. A grid of cells, four rules applied in parallel each tick, no central coordinator. The patterns that emerge — gliders, oscillators, guns, eventually a working universal Turing machine — are the cellular-automaton analogues of the morphogen stripes Turing predicted in 1952.

The math meets, too. Cellular automata are routinely treated as discrete approximations of reaction-diffusion equations. The Greenberg–Hastings automaton models excitable media — heart muscle, slime-mold waves; the numerical PDE solvers for reaction-diffusion are CAs under the hood. The discrete-continuous boundary is porous in both directions.

There's a closure in the arc I find satisfying. Turing's 1936 abstraction — the universal computer — was concretely built inside the discrete cousin of his 1952 abstraction, by Paul Rendell in 2010: a working universal Turing machine, made of gliders, running inside a cellular automaton. Turing 1936 instantiated in Turing 1952's cousin, by people who weren't trying for the closure. The two papers turn out to have been about each other all along.

For biology this is more than aesthetic. Turing-pattern mechanisms have been experimentally confirmed in zebrafish stripes (Kondo & Miura, *Science* 2010), in digit spacing in mouse limbs (Sharpe lab, *Science* 2014), and in hair-follicle distribution. Cellular Potts models, agent-based morphogenesis, the wcEcoli-style whole-cell simulations I work on — all in the cellular-automaton family. Pattern formation in the modern literature is the same project Turing started; we're just sweeping the resolution finer.

If you want to follow this further, two books. Philip Ball's *[The Self-Made Tapestry](https://global.oup.com/academic/product/the-self-made-tapestry-9780198502432)* is the popular treatment, beautifully illustrated, tying Turing to spots, stripes, branching, sand ripples, and honeycombs. James Murray's *[Mathematical Biology](https://link.springer.com/book/10.1007/b98868)* is the rigorous one, the standard textbook for reaction-diffusion in biology, with well over a thousand references — the place to graduate to once you've read Ball.

The companion piece to this would be a [reaction-diffusion playground](#) alongside [/life](/life/) — same idea, continuous instead of discrete, with sliders for the morphogen rate constants. On the list.
